fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the i18n catalog loading runtime we've been talking about. Basically the idea is that developers shouldn't have to hand-roll all that locale file picking, parsing, merging, and key traversal logic every time. We want a single layer that handles all of that.

The system needs to support loading translations from local files in at least two formats (you know, the usual suspects), and also from a remote source abstraction — same output shape either way. When a locale file isn't available, it should gracefully fall back to a default instead of blowing up. We also need that deep-merge behavior we discussed, like what we did with the fallback handling in the login module last time, where the active locale values win but missing keys still come through from the fallback.

For grouped catalog loading, there should be some kind of namespace support, and we need to validate the input before even attempting the load — fail fast, clean error, no stack traces leaking out.

Key resolution needs to handle nested paths using a configurable separator, and plural variants should be selectable by count. There's also a widget rendering scenario where we need to verify the end-to-end visible text output including how object-valued keys behave.

Please make sure the code is well-structured — not everything dumped into one file — and the test harness should be runnable with a single bash command. Cases live under the rcb_tests folder.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for local catalogs we only need to support exactly two formats, JSON (`.json`) and YAML (`.yaml`), and both need to end up in the same output map shape. Which one to use comes from the `format` field in the command input. For locale selection, the default is `en`. If `includeCountry` is true, that becomes `en_US`. If a `locale` string is provided, normalize it by stripping any leading underscore, so `_en` → `en`. That locale identifier is then used directly as the file name when loading catalogs.

On paths, for flat catalogs the asset path is `assets/flutter_i18n/<locale>.<ext>`, like `assets/flutter_i18n/en.json`. For namespaced catalogs it should be `assets/flutter_i18n/<locale>/<namespace>.<ext>`, like `assets/flutter_i18n/en/common.json`. The fallback merge behavior uses a separate asset source named `fallback_merge_asset`, and that resolves to its own subdirectory.

Also to make the fallback behavior extra concrete: if the active locale catalog cannot be loaded, we try the configured fallback catalog. If both fail, return an empty map as `map={}`. If both load, do a deep merge where active leaf values override fallback leaves at the same path, nested maps are merged recursively, and fallback-only leaves stay in the result. That’s the same deep-merge fallback logic called out in Feature 2 (feature2_fallback_deep_merge.json): active locale values override matching fallback keys, nested maps are merged recursively, and fallback-only leaves are preserved in the merged output. In `fallback_merge_asset`, the active `fr` file has `title=flutter_18n_fr` and `block.label2=Bonjour`, while the fallback `en` file has `sub_title=Hello World`, `block.label1=This is my app`, and `block.label2=fallback_label2`. So after merge, `title` comes from fr, `sub_title` comes from en because fr is missing it, `block.label1` comes from en because fr is missing it, and `block.label2` comes from fr because it overrides en.

And just to lock down the test harness details, tests run with `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. The script accepts `--cases-dir <subdir>` and the default is `test_cases`. For each case in each `*.json` file it writes one output file to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<case_index_zero_padded_3>.txt`. For example, the first case of `feature1_flat_catalog_loading.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` writes to `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_flat_catalog_loading@000.txt`. This also lines up with Feature 1 coverage: JSON (`.json`) and YAML (`.yaml`) are the two decoder formats required by Feature 1 and tested in feature1_flat_catalog_loading.json cases 0 and 1.

A few exact details to be precise about: For flat (non-namespaced) catalogs the asset path is `assets/flutter_i18n/<locale>.<ext>` (e.g. `assets/flutter_i18n/en.json`). For namespaced catalogs the path is `assets/flutter_i18n/<locale>/<namespace>.<ext>` (e.g. `assets/flutter_i18n/en/common.json`). The fallback merge feature uses a separate asset source named `fallback_merge_asset`, which resolves to its own subdirectory.

trigger_keywords: how should namespace validation be implemented
what error is returned for empty namespaces
when should namespace errors be raised
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the `namespaces` list is absent or empty in a `namespaced_catalog` command, the system must output exactly `error=invalid_namespaces\n` with no exception traceback or additional text. Validation must happen before any file I/O is attempted.

trigger_keywords: how does dotted key lookup work
what separator is used for nested key traversal
how are missing keys reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Key lookup splits the key string by the provided `separator` and traverses the catalog map depth-first. If the final segment resolves to a string, that value is returned. If the key is missing or resolves to a non-string value (e.g. a nested map), the requested key itself is returned as the translation and the key is added to the `missing_keys` callback array. Missing-key values must never be replaced with booleans.

trigger_keywords: how is plural key selection implemented
what suffix format is used for plural variants
how does the count map to a plural key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Plural variants are stored as `<baseKey>-<count>` (e.g. `keyPlural-1`, `keyPlural-2`). To resolve a plural, the system appends `-<count>` to the base key and performs a standard key lookup. The count is an integer supplied in the command input.

trigger_keywords: what does the submap calculation return
how is the parent map for a nested path computed
what is returned when the path is empty or crosses a non-map node
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The submap is the parent object of the final key segment. For path `object.key1` with separator `.`, all segments except the last (`object`) are traversed, and the map at that node is returned. If the path is empty or any intermediate segment resolves to a non-map value, an empty map `{}` is returned.

trigger_keywords: what texts appear in the widget rendering output
how is the visible_texts array ordered
what is included in the end-to-end rendered text snapshot
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `visible_texts` array is sorted alphabetically and contains: the nested string translation (`Key1Value`), the locale file name (`en`), the key string for an object-valued lookup (`object`), both plural variants (`valuePlural-1`, `valuePlural-2`), and the single translation (`valueSingle`). Object-valued keys render as their requested key rather than a decoded value.

trigger_keywords: what does the remote catalog loader return
how is remote catalog content decoded
what is the output shape for a remote source load
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The remote catalog loader is a stub/abstraction that returns empty content. Its output must be `map={}\n`, using the same `map=<pretty JSON>` shape as local catalog loading. No actual HTTP call is made; the remote source is an injectable abstraction.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Both local and remote catalog loaders must produce `map=<pretty-printed JSON>\n` on stdout, as specified in Features 1 and 5.

context_pointers.vague_reference: fail fast, clean error, no stack traces leaking out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4 namespace validation: output must be exactly `error=invalid_namespaces\n` with no host-language exception text, triggered before any I/O when `namespaces` is absent or empty.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify from the top-level requirement — you'll need to check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or the asset fixture files to get the exact expected behavior.

feature1_flat_catalog_loading.json.description: Loads a single translation catalog and reports the selected catalog identifier, parsed format, and nested entries.

feature2_fallback_deep_merge.json.description: Merges a fallback catalog with the active catalog so active leaf values override fallback leaves while missing fallback leaves remain available.

feature3_namespaced_catalog_loading.json.description: Loads one or more named catalog groups and preserves each group under its own top-level namespace.

feature4_namespace_validation.json.description: Rejects missing or empty namespace lists before attempting to load grouped catalogs.

feature5_remote_catalog_loading.json.description: Loads a catalog through a remote-source loader and decodes the returned content into the same map output shape as local catalogs.

feature6_key_lookup.json.description: Looks up dotted keys in a decoded catalog, returns string leaves, and reports missing-key callbacks without replacing missing values with booleans.

feature7_submap_calculation.json.description: Calculates the parent map used for a nested lookup and returns an empty map when the path is empty or crosses a non-map value.

feature8_plural_lookup.json.description: Selects the pluralized translation key whose numeric suffix matches the requested count as exercised by translated text rendering.

feature9_widget_text_rendering.json.description: Renders translated text widgets using loaded catalog values and leaves object-valued keys visible as their requested key or fallback key.
